params.text:
First aid for a convulsive seizure

When someone has a tonic-clonic (convulsive) seizure, calm and simple first aid keeps them safe until it passes. You cannot stop the seizure, and you do not need to — your job is to protect the person from injury and stay with them. Remember the key actions:

  • Protect from injury: guide them gently and move dangerous objects away.
  • Cushion the head: put something soft under their head.
  • Time it: note when the seizure starts and how long it lasts.

Try to stay calm. Most seizures stop on their own within a few minutes, and your steady presence helps everyone around you stay calm too.

Protecting the person

As soon as you realise someone is having a convulsive seizure, look at the space around them. If they are on the ground, cushion their head with something soft such as a folded cushion or jumper to protect it from the hard floor. Remove harmful objects from nearby, or, if the person cannot be moved, move the hazards away — hot drinks, furniture with sharp corners, or anything they could knock into.

Do not try to move the person unless they are in a dangerous place, such as a road or by a fire. Loosen anything tight around their neck if you can do so easily, and note the time the seizure began. Speak gently and reassuringly, even if they cannot respond — hearing a calm voice can be comforting. Stay with them throughout, and keep other people back so the person has space and privacy.

params.question: A person you support falls and begins a convulsive seizure on the floor. What should you do first?

answers.text: Cushion their head, move nearby objects that could harm them, and note the time

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Protecting the head, clearing hazards and timing the seizure are the right first actions.

answers.text: Hold their arms and legs still to stop the jerking

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. You must never restrain someone during a seizure; protect them from injury instead.

answers.text: Put a spoon or your fingers into their mouth

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. You must never put anything in the person's mouth; cushion their head and clear hazards instead.

answers.text: Lift them onto a chair straight away

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Not quite. Do not move the person unless they are in danger; cushion their head and clear hazards where they are.
